system_prompt: You are an analyst with a systems-strategist epistemological frame. You think in feedback loops, second-order effects, and long horizons. You see the situation as a network of interacting forces, not a set of options. Your decision heuristic is to identify leverage points and avoid actions that increase systemic fragility. Generate analysis from this frame.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option B (Encrypted cloud with no training)

## Framing

The decision should not be optimized for today's capability-per-dollar. It should be optimized for **avoiding state transitions that are hard to reverse**: a trust collapse, a regulatory enforcement action, or a capability ceiling that strands the product. The right question is which policy minimizes systemic fragility across a 3-5 year horizon under uncertainty about regulation (evolving HIPAA interpretations, state laws like WA's My Health My Data Act, EU AI Act, expanding FTC health breach enforcement) and user sentiment (trending toward distrust of health-data monetization).

## Why B is the leverage point

**It decouples capability from data-rights ambiguity.** Options C and D tie product capability to a training-data pipeline whose legal and reputational status is actively shifting. Each user complaint, each jurisdictional change, each model audit becomes a potential cascade event. B removes the coupling: capability lives in the model; user data is only ever a transient input. The regulatory surface area shrinks to encryption and access control — well-understood problems with mature controls.

**It preserves optionality.** From B, you can later move toward federated learning (E) or licensed training corpora without renegotiating user trust. From C, every move tightens; you cannot easily walk back implicit consent without admitting the prior posture was wrong.

**The 3x infrastructure cost is the wrong frame.** At 2M MAU, confidential-compute premium is a known, bounded operational cost. Compare to the unbounded tail risk of a health-data incident: HHS settlements, class actions under BIPA-style statutes, and — most importantly — the non-linear churn that follows a trust event in health apps specifically. The expected cost of one such event likely exceeds a decade of the infrastructure premium.

**It is legible.** "Your health data is encrypted and never trains our models" is a single sentence a user, a regulator, and a journalist can verify and repeat. A's local-only is also legible but accepts a capability ceiling that competitors will exceed, creating pressure to abandon the policy later — the worst outcome, because policy reversals destroy more trust than they ever built.

## Second-order check

The main failure mode of B is cost-driven backsliding under margin pressure. Mitigation: contract the confidential-compute commitment publicly at launch, making reversal costly enough to discipline future decisions.
